sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 18, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: RIGHT green with W center moves horizontally (LEFT/RIGHT) in the corridor. UP didn't work earlier. Need to test if UP works from the current position (27-29).
- Plan: Test UP with right green having W center at col 27-29.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 17 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
UP changed the board (board_changed=True, gameplay_changed=True) but the green squares are still at the same positions! Let me look more carefully at what changed:
- Before: W px=10 at [[63, 54], [63, 63]]
- After: W px=11 at [[63, 53], [63, 63]]
So the white bar at the bottom (row 63) changed from 10 pixels to 11 pixels. This is the HUD/timer bar! The UP action only advanced the timer bar, not the actual gameplay.
Wait, but it says gameplay_changed: True. Hmm, maybe the HUD bar IS considered gameplay? Or maybe something else changed that I'm not seeing.
Actually, looking more carefully, ALL the gameplay objects are at the same positions. The only change is the white bar at row 63 expanding by 1 pixel. So UP with the right green having W center doesn't move anything gameplay-wise - it just advances the timer.
So the mechanics are:
- When LEFT green has W center: UP/DOWN move the left green vertically
- When RIGHT green has W center: LEFT/RIGHT move the right green horizontally
- Other arrow keys with the current active green are no_impact (just advance the timer)
This means:
- Left green can only move UP/DOWN (vertically)
- Right green can only move LEFT/RIGHT (horizontally)
Now, the left green is at (30-32, 18-20) and the left dark outline is at (32-36, 11-15). To get the left green to the left outline, I need to move it DOWN and LEFT. But the left green can only move UP/DOWN, not LEFT/RIGHT!
Similarly, the right green is at (30-32, 27-29) and the right dark outline is at (26-30, 44-48). To get the right green to the right outline, I need to move it RIGHT and UP. But the right green can only move LEFT/RIGHT, not UP/DOWN!
So neither green square can reach its target dark outline with just arrow keys. This suggests the goal is NOT to move green squares into dark outlines.
Let me reconsider what the goal might be:
1. Maybe the goal is to match colors between the green square centers and the dark outline interiors?
2. Maybe the goal is to position the green squares at specific locations (not necessarily inside the outlines)?
3. Maybe MOUSE on the dark outlines does something?
4. Maybe there's a different interaction I haven't discovered?
Let me try clicking on the dark outlines with MOUSE to see if something happens.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the color mechanic. The centers cycle through colors: W, c, B, etc. The dark outlines have white interiors. Maybe the goal is to make both green square centers white (matching the dark outline interiors)?
Currently:
- Left green center: c (charcoal)
- Right green center: W (white)
If I click on the left green center, it swaps with the right green center. So left becomes W and right becomes c. Then both would need to be W for the goal... but that's impossible since they swap.
Unless there's a way to make both W at the same time. Or maybe the goal is to have specific colors in specific positions.
Let me try clicking on the dark outline interiors to see if that does something.
The left dark outline interior is at (33-35, 12-14), center at (34, 13).
The right dark outline interior is at (27-29, 45-47), center at (28, 46).
Let me try MOUSE on (34, 13) - the left dark outline interior.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: UP with right green active only advances the timer bar - no gameplay change. Confirmed: left green moves UP/DOWN, right green moves LEFT/RIGHT. Neither can reach both axes of the dark outlines. Goal may involve color matching or clicking on dark outlines.
Plan: Try MOUSE on the dark outline interiors to discover new mechanics.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the left dark outline interior (34, 13)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 13}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("After MOUSE on (34, 13):")
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] < 50:
        print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
print("  board_changed:", result.get('board_changed'))
print("  gameplay_changed:", result.get('gameplay_changed'))
print("  no_impact:", result.get('no_impact'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
